---
title: "scVelo RNA Velocity Analysis Report"
subtitle: "CD8_NonResponder — Stochastic & Dynamical Models"
author: "ImmunoGene Teqs. Inc by Bin Wu"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    self-contained: true
    fig-align: center
    smooth-scroll: true
    number-sections: true
    title-block-banner: true
    title-block-banner-color: "#0E454A"
    mainfont: "Segoe UI, Helvetica Neue, Arial, sans-serif"
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
          /* === ImmunoGeneTeqs brand colors === */
          :root {
            --igt-teal:       #1D9D9C;
            --igt-dark:       #0E454A;
            --igt-light:      #EDF7F7;
            --igt-accent:     #22B6B4;
            --igt-muted:      #8AAAAD;
          }

          /* === Global spacing === */
          /* Maximize content width - reduce wasted space */
          .quarto-container {
            max-width: 1800px !important;
          }
          #quarto-content > * {
            max-width: 100% !important;
          }
          .page-columns .content {
            max-width: 100% !important;
          }
          main.content {
            max-width: 1800px !important;
          }
          /* Narrower TOC sidebar to give more space to content */
          .sidebar {
            max-width: 180px !important;
          }
          section { margin-bottom: 2.5rem; }

          /* Title block styling */
          .quarto-title-banner {
            background: linear-gradient(135deg, var(--igt-dark) 0%, var(--igt-teal) 100%) !important;
          }
          .quarto-title-banner .quarto-title .title {
            color: #fff !important;
          }
          .quarto-title-banner .quarto-title .subtitle {
            color: var(--igt-light) !important;
          }
          .quarto-title-banner .quarto-title-meta-contents,
          .quarto-title-banner .quarto-title-meta-heading {
            color: var(--igt-light) !important;
          }

          /* Logo in title block */
          .title-logo {
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            gap: 20px;
            margin-bottom: 16px;
            padding: 12px 0;
          }
          .title-logo img {
            height: 40px;
            width: auto;
          }
          .title-logo .logo-divider {
            width: 1px;
            height: 32px;
            background: var(--igt-accent);
          }
          .title-logo span {
            color: var(--igt-dark);
            font-size: 1.05rem;
            font-weight: 400;
            letter-spacing: 0.3px;
          }

          /* Headings */
          h1, h2, h3, h4 {
            color: var(--igt-dark) !important;
          }
          h2 {
            border-bottom: 3px solid var(--igt-teal);
            padding-bottom: 6px;
            margin-top: 2rem;
          }
          h3 {
            border-left: 4px solid var(--igt-accent);
            padding-left: 12px;
            margin-top: 1.5rem;
          }

          /* TOC sidebar */
          .sidebar nav[role=doc-toc] a {
            color: var(--igt-dark);
          }
          .sidebar nav[role=doc-toc] a:hover,
          .sidebar nav[role=doc-toc] a.active {
            color: var(--igt-teal) !important;
            border-left-color: var(--igt-teal) !important;
          }

          /* Links */
          a { color: var(--igt-teal); }
          a:hover { color: var(--igt-dark); }

          /* === FIGURE styling === */
          .quarto-figure {
            border: 1px solid #d8e8e8;
            border-radius: 12px;
            padding: 20px 20px 10px 20px;
            box-shadow: 0 2px 12px rgba(14,69,74,0.08);
            margin-bottom: 28px;
            background: #fff;
            transition: box-shadow 0.25s ease, transform 0.25s ease;
          }
          .quarto-figure:hover {
            box-shadow: 0 6px 24px rgba(14,69,74,0.14);
            transform: translateY(-2px);
          }
          .quarto-figure img {
            border-radius: 6px;
            display: block;
            margin: 0 auto;
            max-width: 100%;
            height: auto;
            cursor: zoom-in;
            background: #fff;
            border: 1px solid #edf4f4;
          }
          /* Figure captions */
          figcaption, .figure-caption {
            color: var(--igt-muted);
            font-style: normal;
            font-size: 0.98rem;
            margin-top: 10px;
            padding-top: 8px;
            border-top: 1px solid #e8f0f0;
            text-align: center;
            line-height: 1.5;
          }

          /* Multi-panel layout spacing */
          .quarto-layout-panel {
            gap: 20px !important;
          }
          .quarto-layout-row {
            gap: 16px !important;
            margin-bottom: 16px;
          }

          /* Code blocks */
          pre.sourceCode {
            border-left: 4px solid var(--igt-accent);
            background: var(--igt-light) !important;
            border-radius: 8px;
            padding: 14px;
          }

          /* === TABLE styling === */
          .cell-output-display {
            overflow-x: auto;
            margin: 20px auto;
            max-width: 100%;
          }
          table, .dataframe, .table {
            width: 100% !important;
            max-width: 1100px;
            margin: 12px auto !important;
            border-collapse: separate !important;
            border-spacing: 0 !important;
            border-radius: 10px;
            overflow: hidden;
            box-shadow: 0 2px 12px rgba(14,69,74,0.08);
            font-size: 0.88rem;
          }
          table thead, .dataframe thead, .table > thead {
            background: var(--igt-dark) !important;
          }
          table thead th, .dataframe thead th, .table > thead th {
            color: #fff !important;
            font-weight: 600 !important;
            padding: 12px 16px !important;
            text-align: left;
            font-size: 0.85rem;
            letter-spacing: 0.3px;
            border-bottom: 2px solid var(--igt-teal) !important;
          }
          table tbody td, .dataframe tbody td {
            padding: 10px 16px !important;
            border-bottom: 1px solid #e8f0f0 !important;
            vertical-align: middle;
            color: #1a1a1a !important;
          }
          table tbody th, .dataframe tbody th {
            padding: 10px 16px !important;
            border-bottom: 1px solid #e8f0f0 !important;
            font-weight: 600;
            background: #d4eded !important;
            color: var(--igt-dark) !important;
          }
          table tbody tr:nth-child(even),
          .dataframe tbody tr:nth-child(even) {
            background: #f7fbfb;
          }
          table tbody tr:nth-child(odd),
          .dataframe tbody tr:nth-child(odd) {
            background: #fff;
          }
          table tbody tr:hover,
          .dataframe tbody tr:hover {
            background: var(--igt-light) !important;
            transition: background 0.2s ease;
          }
          table tbody tr:last-child td,
          .dataframe tbody tr:last-child td {
            border-bottom: none !important;
          }

          /* Section dividers */
          hr {
            border-top: 2px solid var(--igt-accent);
            opacity: 0.3;
            margin: 2.5rem 0;
          }

          /* Footer */
          .quarto-footer {
            background: var(--igt-dark);
            color: var(--igt-light);
          }

          /* Callout boxes */
          .callout-note {
            border-left-color: var(--igt-teal) !important;
            border-radius: 8px;
          }
          .callout {
            border-radius: 8px;
            margin: 1.5rem 0;
          }

          /* Python output images (from IPython.display) - LARGE */
          .cell-output img {
            border-radius: 6px;
            box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(14,69,74,0.06);
            margin: 12px auto;
            display: block;
            max-width: 100%;
            width: 100%;
            cursor: zoom-in;
            background: #fff;
            border: 1px solid #edf4f4;
          }
          /* Description paragraphs before figures */
          .description {
            background: var(--igt-light);
            border-left: 4px solid var(--igt-accent);
            padding: 14px 18px;
            border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
            margin: 18px 0;
            font-size: 1.0rem;
            line-height: 1.6;
            color: #333;
          }
          .how-to-read {
            background: #fff;
            border: 1px solid #d8e8e8;
            border-left: 4px solid var(--igt-teal);
            padding: 14px 18px;
            border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
            margin: 14px 0;
            font-size: 0.98rem;
            line-height: 1.6;
            color: #444;
          }
          .how-to-read strong {
            color: var(--igt-dark);
          }

          /* Improve readability on laptop/mobile widths */
          @media (max-width: 1400px) {
            .quarto-container,
            main.content {
              max-width: 1300px !important;
            }
            .sidebar {
              max-width: 220px !important;
            }
          }
          @media (max-width: 900px) {
            figcaption, .figure-caption {
              font-size: 0.92rem;
            }
            .description, .how-to-read {
              font-size: 0.92rem;
            }
          }
        </style>
        <script>
          document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", function () {
            const imgs = document.querySelectorAll(".quarto-figure img, .cell-output img");
            imgs.forEach(function (img) {
              if (!img.src || img.closest("a")) return;
              const link = document.createElement("a");
              link.href = img.src;
              link.target = "_blank";
              link.rel = "noopener noreferrer";
              img.parentNode.insertBefore(link, img);
              link.appendChild(img);
              if (!img.title) img.title = "Click to open full-resolution image";
            });
          });
        </script>
bibliography: references.bib
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: scvelo_env
---

<div class="title-logo">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="ImmunoGeneTeqs" style="height:40px;width:auto;">
  <div class="logo-divider"></div>
  <span>Leading Next-Gen Sequencing</span>
</div>

## Overview

This report summarizes RNA velocity analysis of the **CD8_NonResponder** dataset
(1,631 cells) using a Scanpy/scVelo workflow [@Wolf2018; @Bergen2020].
RNA velocity was inferred from spliced and unspliced transcript abundances
following the framework of @LaManno2018 and its dynamical extension by
@Bergen2020.

The splicing kinetics were modeled as [@Bergen2020]:

$$
\frac{du(t)}{dt} = \alpha_{k}(t) - \beta\, u(t), \qquad
\frac{ds(t)}{dt} = \beta\, u(t) - \gamma\, s(t)
$$

where $u(t)$ and $s(t)$ are unspliced and spliced mRNA, respectively, and
$\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ denote transcription, splicing, and degradation
parameters. scVelo provides deterministic, stochastic, and dynamical velocity
formulations [@Bergen2020]. This report uses stochastic
(steady-state approximation) and dynamical (likelihood-based EM) models, with
downstream analyses including velocity confidence, velocity pseudotime, latent
time, and PAGA-based directed transitions [@LaManno2018; @Bergen2020; @Wolf2019].

::: {.callout-note}
## Analysis Environment
**scVelo** 0.3.3 [@Bergen2020] | **scanpy** 1.9.3 [@Wolf2018] | **Python** 3.9 | macOS Apple M4 Pro
:::

```{python}
#| label: load-data
#| echo: true

import scanpy as sc
import scvelo as scv
import pandas as pd
import re
from pathlib import Path
from IPython.display import HTML

def _cluster_sort_key(x):
    s = str(x)
    return (0, int(s)) if re.fullmatch(r"\d+", s) else (1, s)

RESULTS = Path("CD8_NonResponder/results")

# Load the final dynamical h5ad (contains both stochastic & dynamical layers)
adata = sc.read_h5ad(RESULTS / "data" / "CD8_NonResponder_dynamical.h5ad")
print(f"Cells: {adata.n_obs:,}  |  Genes: {adata.n_vars:,}")
print(f"Layers: {list(adata.layers.keys())}")
print(f"Cluster variable: seurat_clusters ({adata.obs['seurat_clusters'].nunique()} clusters)")
print(f"Embeddings: {[k for k in adata.obsm if k.startswith('X_')]}")
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Figure Review Tip
Click any figure to open the full-resolution PNG in a new browser tab.
This is useful for dense multi-panel plots where gene names are hard to read inline.
:::

## Brief Methods

The final AnnData object was initialized from the `spliced` layer when available.
Before velocity fitting, genes were excluded using species-specific rules
implemented in `scripts/run_scvelo_pipeline.py`:

1. **Human symbols**
   Mitochondrial: `MT-*`; ribosomal: `^RPL\\d`, `^RPS\\d`, `^RPLP\\d`;
   pseudogene-like symbols: `^RPL\\d+P\\d+$`, `^RPS\\d+P\\d+$`, `-ps`.
2. **Mouse symbols**
   Mitochondrial: `mt-*`; ribosomal: `^Rpl\\d`, `^Rps\\d`, `^Rplp\\d`;
   pseudogene-like symbols: `-ps`.

For both species, genes annotated with `pseudogene` in available biotype
columns were also excluded.

After filtering, data were processed with
`scv.pp.filter_and_normalize(min_shared_counts=20, n_top_genes=2000)` and
`scv.pp.moments(n_pcs=<n_pcs>, n_neighbors=<n_neighbors>)` (defaults:
`n_pcs=30`, `n_neighbors=30`) prior to velocity inference [@Bergen2020]. These
defaults match the official scVelo stochastic/dynamical tutorials and are
appropriate baseline settings for datasets of this size; `n_neighbors` can be
tuned upward for smoother global trajectories or downward for higher local
sensitivity (see:
[RNA Velocity Basics](https://scvelo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/VelocityBasics.html),
[Dynamical Modeling](https://scvelo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/DynamicalModeling.html)).

Stochastic velocity was then computed using `scv.tl.velocity` and
`scv.tl.velocity_graph`, followed by velocity confidence metrics and velocity
pseudotime [@LaManno2018; @Bergen2020]. Dynamical modeling was performed with
`scv.tl.recover_dynamics`, `scv.tl.velocity(mode='dynamical')`, and
`scv.tl.latent_time` to estimate kinetic parameters and shared latent time
[@Bergen2020]. Directed cluster transitions were summarized by
velocity-informed PAGA [@Wolf2019].

For parameter suitability, the preprocessing defaults used here
(`min_shared_counts=20`, `n_top_genes=2000`, `n_pcs=30`, `n_neighbors=30`)
match the official scVelo tutorial settings and fall within documented
neighbor-graph guidance (`n_neighbors` typically 2–100). For this dataset
size (~5k cells), these values are suitable as a baseline; tuning should be
considered when prioritizing rare-state sensitivity (lower neighbors) versus
global trajectory smoothness (higher neighbors).

---

## QC: Spliced / Unspliced Proportions

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> Before running velocity estimation, we examine the
proportions of <strong>spliced</strong> (mature mRNA), <strong>unspliced</strong>
(pre-mRNA with introns), and <strong>ambiguous</strong> reads for each cell.
This quality control step verifies that sufficient unspliced reads are present for
velocity estimation — typically 10–25% of total counts depending on the sequencing
protocol.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read this figure:</strong> Each bar represents one cell cluster.
The bar segments show the proportion of spliced (blue), unspliced (orange), and ambiguous
(green) reads. Higher unspliced proportions indicate more active transcription.
Variations across clusters reflect differences in transcriptional activity and
splicing efficiency at different stages of differentiation.
</div>

![Proportions of spliced, unspliced, and ambiguous reads per cell, grouped by cluster.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-01-proportions.png){#fig-proportions}

---

## Part 1 — Stochastic Velocity

<div class="description">
Part 1 used the stochastic velocity model (`scv.tl.velocity`) followed by
construction of the velocity transition graph (`scv.tl.velocity_graph`)
[@LaManno2018; @Bergen2020]. Under the steady-state approximation, per-gene
velocity residuals are estimated from the unspliced-spliced relationship:

$$v_{ij} = u_{ij} - \hat{\gamma}_i\, s_{ij}$$

where $\hat{\gamma}_i$ is the fitted degradation-to-splicing ratio. The
second-order moment extension was enabled in scVelo to improve robust
steady-state estimation across heterogeneous cell states [@Bergen2020].
</div>

### Velocity Stream Plots

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> The velocity vectors are projected onto low-dimensional
embeddings (UMAP and FItSNE) using <code>scv.pl.velocity_embedding_stream()</code>.
The streamlines show the <strong>average direction and speed of cell state change</strong>
across the embedding space, computed from the velocity-weighted transition probabilities.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> Each streamline arrow indicates the
<strong>predicted direction of differentiation</strong>. Follow the arrows to trace the
flow of transcriptional change — from progenitors toward differentiated cell states.
Cells are coloured by <strong>seurat_clusters</strong> (cluster identity) or
<strong>TagIDs</strong> (sample/condition). Regions where streamlines converge indicate
differentiation endpoints; regions where they diverge suggest branching or
progenitor states. Thicker/denser streamlines indicate higher velocity (faster
transcriptional change).
</div>

::: {layout-ncol=1}

![UMAP — seurat_clusters](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-02-stoch-stream-umap-seurat-clusters.png){#fig-stoch-stream-umap-sc group="stoch-stream"}

![UMAP — orig.ident](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/stoch-stream-umap-orig-ident.png){#fig-stoch-stream-umap-orig group="stoch-stream"}

![UMAP — TagIDs](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-03-stoch-stream-umap-TagIDs.png){#fig-stoch-stream-umap-tag group="stoch-stream"}

![FItSNE — seurat_clusters](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-04-stoch-stream-fitsne-seurat-clusters.png){#fig-stoch-stream-fitsne-sc group="stoch-stream"}

![FItSNE — orig.ident](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/stoch-stream-fitsne-orig-ident.png){#fig-stoch-stream-fitsne-orig group="stoch-stream"}

![FItSNE — TagIDs](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-05-stoch-stream-fitsne-TagIDs.png){#fig-stoch-stream-fitsne-tag group="stoch-stream"}

:::

### Phase Portraits — Top Velocity Genes

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> Phase portraits display the relationship between
<strong>spliced (x-axis)</strong> and <strong>unspliced (y-axis)</strong> mRNA expression
for the top 8 genes ranked by velocity fit residual. The function
<code>scv.pl.velocity()</code> generates these plots with a fitted steady-state regression line.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read this figure:</strong> The <strong>black diagonal line</strong>
represents the estimated steady-state ratio of unspliced to spliced mRNA.
Cells <strong>above the line</strong> have more unspliced mRNA than expected
→ the gene is being <strong>actively transcribed (up-regulated, positive velocity)</strong>.
Cells <strong>below the line</strong> have less unspliced mRNA than expected
→ transcription has slowed or stopped (<strong>down-regulated, negative velocity</strong>).
The colour of each dot indicates the cell's cluster identity. A gene that shows many cells far from the steady-state line is a strong contributor to the velocity field.
</div>

![Phase portraits (spliced vs unspliced) for the top 8 genes by velocity fit residual. The black line is the fitted steady-state ratio.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-06-phase-portraits.png){#fig-phase}

### Velocity Confidence

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> Two complementary metrics are calculated using
<code>scv.tl.velocity_confidence()</code>:
<ul>
<li><strong>Velocity length</strong> — the magnitude (Euclidean norm) of the velocity vector.
This measures the <strong>speed or rate of differentiation</strong>: how rapidly a cell
is changing its transcriptional state.</li>
<li><strong>Velocity confidence</strong> — the correlation of each cell's velocity vector
with the velocities of its neighbours. This measures the <strong>coherence</strong> of the
vector field: high confidence means neighbours agree on direction, low confidence
indicates an ambiguous or transitional state.</li>
</ul>
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> In the UMAP plots, each cell is coloured by
velocity length (left) or confidence (right) using a coolwarm colourmap. <strong>Red/warm
cells</strong> are differentiating rapidly (high speed) or have highly coherent velocity
(high confidence). <strong>Blue/cool cells</strong> are quiescent or have ambiguous
direction. The combined scatter plot (below) shows both metrics simultaneously — cells
in the upper-right corner are both fast-moving and directionally coherent.
</div>

::: {layout-ncol=1}

![Speed (velocity length) — magnitude of velocity vector per cell](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-07-velocity-length-umap.png){#fig-vlen group="confidence"}

![Coherence (velocity confidence) — directional agreement with neighbouring cells](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-08-velocity-confidence-umap.png){#fig-vconf group="confidence"}

:::

![Combined velocity length (x-axis) & confidence (y-axis) scatter per cell.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-09-velocity-confidence-combined.png){#fig-combined-conf}

<div class="description">
<strong>Table below:</strong> Mean velocity length and confidence aggregated by cluster.
The coolwarm colour gradient highlights which clusters differentiate fastest (high velocity
length) and which have the most coherent velocity direction (high confidence).
Clusters with high speed but low coherence may be at lineage branching points.
</div>

```{python}
#| label: tbl-confidence
#| tbl-cap: "Mean velocity length and confidence by cluster"

# Regenerate from adata with numeric cluster ordering
conf_df = (
    adata.obs.groupby("seurat_clusters", observed=True)
    .agg(velocity_length=("velocity_length", "mean"),
         velocity_confidence=("velocity_confidence", "mean"))
    .round(4)
    .T
)
# Sort numerically when possible, then lexicographically
conf_df = conf_df[sorted(conf_df.columns, key=_cluster_sort_key)]
HTML(
    conf_df.style
    .background_gradient(axis=1, cmap="coolwarm")
    .format(precision=4)
    .set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th",
         "props": [("background-color", "#0E454A"),
                   ("color", "#fff"),
                   ("font-weight", "bold"),
                   ("padding", "12px 16px"),
                   ("font-size", "0.85rem")]},
        {"selector": "td",
         "props": [("border-bottom", "1px solid #e8f0f0"),
                   ("padding", "10px 16px")]},
        {"selector": "",
         "props": [("border-radius", "10px"),
                   ("overflow", "hidden"),
                   ("box-shadow", "0 2px 12px rgba(14,69,74,0.08)")]},
    ])
    .to_html()
)
```

### Cell-Cell Transition Network

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> The <strong>velocity graph</strong>
(<code>scv.pl.velocity_graph()</code>) visualises all velocity-inferred cell-to-cell
transitions. For each cell, cosine correlation between its velocity vector and the
displacement vectors to all other cells determines the transition probabilities.
Only high-probability transitions (threshold applied) are drawn as edges.
The <strong>cell transition plot</strong> (<code>scv.utils.get_cell_transitions()</code>)
traces a selected cell through the Markov chain to show its predicted trajectory.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> In the <strong>velocity graph</strong> (left),
thin lines connect cells that the velocity field predicts will transition between each
other. Dense connections within a cluster indicate internal cycling or self-renewal;
connections between clusters show differentiation routes. In the <strong>cell
transitions</strong> plot (right), coloured dots trace the predicted path of a starting
cell through the graph (colour gradient from dark to light = ascending probability).
</div>

::: {layout-ncol=1}

![Velocity graph — cell-to-cell transitions with high velocity-transition probability](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-10-velocity-graph-umap.png){#fig-vgraph-umap group="vgraph"}

![Cell transitions — tracing a cell's predicted fate through the velocity graph](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-11-cell-transitions-umap.png){#fig-transitions-umap group="vgraph"}

:::

### Velocity Pseudotime

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> <code>scv.tl.velocity_pseudotime()</code> infers
a <strong>pseudotemporal ordering</strong> of cells based on the velocity graph.
Starting from automatically identified root cells (cells with high probability of being
at the beginning of the trajectory), it measures the average number of steps along the
directed velocity graph needed to reach each cell. Unlike diffusion pseudotime (which uses
an undirected similarity kernel), velocity pseudotime is based on the <strong>directed</strong>
velocity graph and implicitly identifies root cells.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> Each cell is coloured on a continuous gnuplot
colourmap from <strong>dark (early / root, pseudotime ≈ 0)</strong> to
<strong>bright yellow (late / terminal, pseudotime ≈ 1)</strong>.
The gradient across the embedding reveals the inferred temporal order of
differentiation. Smooth gradients indicate a clear trajectory; abrupt colour
changes suggest rapid transitions or branching events.
</div>

::: {layout-ncol=1}

![Pseudotime — UMAP](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-12-pseudotime-umap.png){#fig-ptime-umap group="ptime"}

![Pseudotime — FItSNE](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-13-pseudotime-fitsne.png){#fig-ptime-fitsne group="ptime"}

:::

### PAGA Velocity Graph

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> <strong>PAGA (Partition-based Graph Abstraction; @Wolf2019)</strong>
is a top-performing trajectory inference method that generates a coarse-grained,
graph-like map of the data topology. Here, PAGA is extended with velocity-inferred
<strong>directionality</strong> using <code>scv.tl.paga()</code>. This collapses the
cell-level velocity graph into <strong>directed edges between clusters</strong>, where edge
weights reflect the transition confidence between cluster pairs. This provides a
simplified view of the major differentiation routes.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read the figure:</strong> Each node represents a cluster, positioned on the
UMAP embedding. <strong>Directed arrows</strong> between nodes indicate predicted
differentiation transitions, with <strong>thicker arrows = higher transition
confidence</strong>. Clusters with no outgoing arrows are predicted terminal states;
clusters with no incoming arrows are predicted root/progenitor states.
</div>

![PAGA directed cluster-cluster transition graph superimposed onto the UMAP embedding. Arrow thickness reflects transition confidence.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/stochastic/fig-14-paga-umap.png){#fig-paga}

<div class="description">
<strong>Table below:</strong> The PAGA transition confidence matrix. Each cell $(i, j)$
shows the confidence that cells in cluster $i$ (row) transition to cluster $j$ (column).
The matrix reads <strong>left-to-right: row → column</strong>. Values near 0 indicate
no predicted transition; higher values (darker blue in the colour gradient) indicate
confident transitions. This quantifies the arrows shown in the PAGA figure above.
</div>

```{python}
#| label: tbl-paga
#| tbl-cap: "PAGA transition confidence matrix (row → column)"

# Load pre-generated PAGA table and re-sort numerically
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup as _BS
_paga_raw = (RESULTS / "tables" / "tbl-02-paga-transitions.html").read_text()
_soup = _BS(_paga_raw, "html.parser")
_tbl = _soup.find("table")
_ths = [th.get_text(strip=True) for th in _tbl.find("thead").find_all("th")]
_cols = [h for h in _ths if h]
_data = {}
for _tr in _tbl.find("tbody").find_all("tr"):
    _lbl = _tr.find("th").get_text(strip=True)
    _vals = [float(td.get_text(strip=True)) if td.get_text(strip=True) else 0.0
             for td in _tr.find_all("td")]
    _data[_lbl] = dict(zip(_cols, _vals))
_paga_df = pd.DataFrame(_data).T
_order = sorted(_paga_df.index, key=_cluster_sort_key)
_paga_df = _paga_df.loc[_order, _order].round(4)
HTML(
    _paga_df.style
    .background_gradient(cmap="Blues", vmin=0)
    .format(precision=4)
    .set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th",
         "props": [("background-color", "#0E454A"),
                   ("color", "#fff"),
                   ("font-weight", "bold"),
                   ("padding", "12px 16px"),
                   ("font-size", "0.85rem")]},
        {"selector": "td",
         "props": [("border-bottom", "1px solid #e8f0f0"),
                   ("padding", "10px 16px")]},
        {"selector": "",
         "props": [("border-radius", "10px"),
                   ("overflow", "hidden"),
                   ("box-shadow", "0 2px 12px rgba(14,69,74,0.08)")]},
    ])
    .to_html()
)
```

---

## Part 2 — Dynamical Modeling

<div class="description">
Part 2 applied the dynamical model using `scv.tl.recover_dynamics()` and
`scv.tl.velocity(mode='dynamical')` [@Bergen2020]. Unlike the stochastic
approach, this model fits full transcriptional kinetics:

$$\frac{du(t)}{dt} = \alpha_{k}(t) - \beta\, u(t), \qquad \frac{ds(t)}{dt} = \beta\, u(t) - \gamma\, s(t)$$

in a likelihood-based EM framework to estimate gene-wise kinetic parameters
($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$), switching times, and a shared latent time axis.
The inferred latent time was used as an embedding-independent temporal ordering
for downstream interpretation and cross-validation of trajectory directionality
[@Bergen2020].
</div>

### Kinetic Rate Distributions

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> After fitting the dynamical model to all velocity genes,
the estimated transcription ($\alpha$), splicing ($\beta$), and degradation ($\gamma$)
rates are extracted from <code>adata.var</code>. These histograms summarize the genome-wide
distribution of each kinetic parameter.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read this figure:</strong> Each histogram shows the distribution of one
kinetic rate across all fitted genes (log scale on x-axis). The
<strong>transcription rate ($\alpha$)</strong> governs how fast unspliced mRNA is produced;
<strong>splicing rate ($\beta$)</strong> controls how fast unspliced becomes spliced;
<strong>degradation rate ($\gamma$)</strong> determines mature mRNA turnover speed.
Genes with high $\alpha$ and low $\gamma$ will have high steady-state expression.
The ratio $\beta/\gamma$ determines whether splicing or degradation is the
rate-limiting step.
</div>

![Histograms of estimated transcription ($\alpha$), splicing ($\beta$), and degradation ($\gamma$) rates across all velocity genes.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-15-kinetic-rates.png){#fig-kinetics}

### Velocity Stream Plots (Dynamical)

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> After computing the dynamical velocity and rebuilding
the velocity graph, streamlines are again projected onto UMAP and FItSNE embeddings.
The dynamical model typically yields more refined directionality than the stochastic model,
especially for genes where the steady-state assumption breaks down (e.g., genes with
multi-state dynamics or rapid induction/repression switches).
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> Interpretation is the same as the stochastic
streamlines above: follow the arrows to see the predicted direction of differentiation.
<strong>Compare these with the stochastic stream plots</strong> (Part 1) — regions where
both models agree provide the strongest evidence for the inferred trajectory direction.
Differences highlight where the full dynamical treatment resolves ambiguities that the
steady-state approximation cannot.
</div>

::: {layout-ncol=1}

![UMAP — seurat_clusters](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-16-dyn-stream-umap-seurat-clusters.png){#fig-dyn-stream-umap-sc group="dyn-stream"}

![UMAP — orig.ident](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/dyn-stream-umap-orig-ident.png){#fig-dyn-stream-umap-orig group="dyn-stream"}

![UMAP — TagIDs](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-17-dyn-stream-umap-TagIDs.png){#fig-dyn-stream-umap-tag group="dyn-stream"}

![FItSNE — seurat_clusters](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-18-dyn-stream-fitsne-seurat-clusters.png){#fig-dyn-stream-fitsne-sc group="dyn-stream"}

![FItSNE — orig.ident](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/dyn-stream-fitsne-orig-ident.png){#fig-dyn-stream-fitsne-orig group="dyn-stream"}

![FItSNE — TagIDs](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-19-dyn-stream-fitsne-TagIDs.png){#fig-dyn-stream-fitsne-tag group="dyn-stream"}

:::

### Latent Time

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> <code>scv.tl.latent_time()</code> recovers a
<strong>shared latent time</strong> across all cells, representing the cell's internal
biological clock. This is derived solely from the gene-level kinetic fits — it is
computed independently of any embedding coordinates. The latent time aligns gene-specific
switching times into a common temporal axis, enabling ordering of cells along the
trajectory from the earliest progenitors (latent time ≈ 0) to the most differentiated
cells (latent time ≈ 1).
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> Each cell is coloured by its inferred latent
time on a continuous gnuplot colourmap (<strong>dark = early, yellow = late</strong>).
When the latent time gradient aligns with a visible gradient across the UMAP/FItSNE
embedding, this provides <strong>strong orthogonal evidence</strong> that the
low-dimensional representation faithfully captures the underlying biological trajectory.
Clusters that appear adjacent in UMAP but differ markedly in latent time may indicate
rapid transitions involving large regulatory changes —
a pattern frequently seen at lineage commitment points.
Below, the same UMAP is also coloured by cluster identity, sample origin, and TagIDs
to cross-reference latent time with biological covariates.
</div>

![Latent time — UMAP (dark = early, yellow = late)](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-20-latent-time-umap.png){#fig-latent-umap group="latent"}

![Latent time — FItSNE](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-21-latent-time-fitsne.png){#fig-latent-fitsne group="latent"}

::: {layout-ncol=1}

![seurat_clusters](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-20a-latent-time-umap-seurat-clusters.png){group="latent-cvar"}

![orig.ident](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-20b-latent-time-umap-orig-ident.png){group="latent-cvar"}

:::

![TagIDs](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-20c-latent-time-umap-TagIDs.png){group="latent-cvar"}

### Top Likelihood Genes

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> Genes are ranked by their <strong>dynamical model
fit likelihood</strong> (<code>adata.var['fit_likelihood']</code>). The top 15
highest-likelihood genes are plotted as phase portraits using
<code>scv.pl.scatter(basis=top_genes)</code>. These are the <strong>driver genes</strong>
that exhibit the most pronounced, well-characterised dynamic behaviour — their
unspliced/spliced trajectories are well explained by the dynamical model.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read this figure:</strong> Each sub-panel shows one gene's phase portrait
(spliced on x-axis, unspliced on y-axis). The <strong>fitted curve</strong> traces the
expected trajectory from gene induction (rising unspliced and spliced)
through the switch point to repression (declining unspliced, then declining spliced).
Cells that follow this curve tightly confirm the model fit. These genes are the
strongest contributors to the velocity field and most reliable for trajectory inference.
</div>

![Phase portraits for the 15 genes with highest dynamical model likelihood. Each panel shows one gene's spliced (x) vs unspliced (y) expression with the fitted dynamical trajectory.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-22-top-likelihood-genes.png){#fig-top-likelihood}

### Gene Expression Dynamics

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> For the top driver genes, two complementary views are
generated: (1) <strong>phase portraits</strong> showing spliced vs unspliced expression,
and (2) <strong>expression over latent time</strong> showing how each gene's expression
level changes along the inferred trajectory.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong>
<ul>
<li><strong>Left panel (phase portrait):</strong> Spliced (x) vs unspliced (y) expression
coloured by cluster. The curve traces the full transcriptional cycle: induction → steady
state → repression.</li>
<li><strong>Right panel (expression vs latent time):</strong> X-axis is the inferred
latent time (0 = earliest, 1 = latest). Y-axis is gene expression. This reveals
<strong>when</strong> along the trajectory each gene peaks, declines, or is activated —
directly showing the temporal program of gene regulation.</li>
</ul>
</div>

![Spliced vs unspliced expression for top driver genes.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-23-gene-expression-phase.png){#fig-gene-phase group="gene-dyn"}

![Expression over latent time for top driver genes — revealing when each gene is activated or repressed.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-24-gene-expression-latent-time.png){#fig-gene-latent group="gene-dyn"}

### Heatmap — Top 150 Genes by Latent Time

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> The top 150 genes ranked by dynamical model likelihood
are sorted by their peak expression time along the latent time axis and plotted as a
heatmap using <code>scv.pl.heatmap(sortby='latent_time')</code>.
A convolution window smooths the expression profile along the time axis.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read this figure:</strong>
<ul>
<li><strong>X-axis (horizontal):</strong> cells ordered by latent time
(left = early/progenitor, right = late/differentiated).</li>
<li><strong>Y-axis (vertical):</strong> genes sorted by peak expression time.</li>
<li><strong>Colour intensity:</strong> red/warm = high expression, blue/cool = low expression.</li>
<li><strong>Colour bar at top:</strong> cluster identity of each cell, allowing you to see
which clusters dominate at each time point.</li>
</ul>
The cascading pattern of expression waves from top-left to bottom-right reveals the
<strong>temporal program of transcriptional regulation</strong>: early genes that drive
the initial differentiation signal, intermediate genes at lineage commitment, and late
genes characterising terminal cell states.
</div>

![Top 150 genes sorted by peak expression time along latent time. Cascading waves reveal the temporal transcriptional program.](CD8_NonResponder/results/figures/dynamical/fig-25-heatmap-top-genes.png){#fig-heatmap}

### Dynamical Top Genes per Cluster

<div class="description">
<strong>What was computed:</strong> <code>scv.tl.rank_dynamical_genes()</code>
computes <strong>cluster-specific partial gene likelihoods</strong>, identifying which
genes are most dynamically active within each cluster. For each cluster,
the top 5 genes are shown as phase portraits, enabling identification of
<strong>cluster-specific driver genes</strong> that characterise the transcriptional
dynamics unique to each cell state.
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read these figures:</strong> Each row corresponds to one cluster.
Within each row, the 5 sub-panels show the top-ranking genes for that cluster.
Each sub-panel is a phase portrait (spliced vs unspliced) with the fitted dynamical
trajectory. Genes that appear for a given cluster but not others indicate
<strong>cluster-specific regulatory programs</strong> driving differentiation at that
particular stage.
</div>

```{python}
#| label: fig-dyn-top-genes

from IPython.display import display, HTML as IPyHTML

dyn_fig = RESULTS / "figures" / "dynamical"
for fig in sorted(dyn_fig.glob("fig-26-dyn-top-genes-*.png")):
    display(IPyHTML(
        f'<div>'
        f'<img src="{fig.as_posix()}" alt="{fig.name}" '
        f'style="width:100%;height:auto;display:block;">'
        f'</div>'
    ))
```

---

## Cell Metadata Summary

<div class="description">
<strong>What is shown:</strong> The first 20 cells from the dataset with their
associated metadata: cluster assignment, sample origin, TagID (condition label),
velocity pseudotime (from the stochastic model), latent time (from the dynamical model),
velocity length (differentiation speed), and velocity confidence (directional coherence).
</div>

<div class="how-to-read">
<strong>How to read this table:</strong> Each row is one cell (barcode).
The <strong>YlOrRd colour gradient</strong> on pseudotime and latent time columns highlights
temporal ordering — yellow = early, red = late. Comparing
<strong>velocity_pseudotime</strong> (stochastic) with <strong>latent_time</strong>
(dynamical) reveals whether the two models agree on cell ordering. Discrepancies may
highlight cells where the steady-state assumption breaks down and the dynamical model
provides a more accurate estimate.
</div>

```{python}
#| label: tbl-metadata
#| tbl-cap: "Cell metadata summary — first 20 cells"

cols = ["seurat_clusters", "orig.ident", "TagIDs",
        "velocity_pseudotime", "latent_time",
        "velocity_length", "velocity_confidence"]
avail = [c for c in cols if c in adata.obs.columns]
df = adata.obs[avail].head(20).copy()

# Round numeric columns
for c in df.select_dtypes("number").columns:
    df[c] = df[c].round(4)

HTML(
    df.style
    .format(precision=4)
    .background_gradient(subset=["velocity_pseudotime", "latent_time"],
                         cmap="YlOrRd")
    .set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th",
         "props": [("background-color", "#0E454A"),
                   ("color", "#fff"),
                   ("font-weight", "bold"),
                   ("padding", "12px 16px"),
                   ("font-size", "0.85rem")]},
        {"selector": "td",
         "props": [("border-bottom", "1px solid #e8f0f0"),
                   ("padding", "10px 16px")]},
        {"selector": "",
         "props": [("border-radius", "10px"),
                   ("overflow", "hidden"),
                   ("box-shadow", "0 2px 12px rgba(14,69,74,0.08)")]},
    ])
    .to_html()
)
```

<div class="description">
<strong>Table below:</strong> Per-cluster summary statistics showing the mean pseudotime,
latent time, and velocity confidence for each cluster. The <strong>viridis gradient</strong>
on latent time and <strong>RdYlGn gradient</strong> on confidence highlight the temporal
and directional properties of each cluster. Clusters with early latent time are progenitors;
those with late latent time are terminally differentiated. High confidence indicates clear,
unambiguous differentiation direction within that cluster.
</div>

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cluster-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Per-cluster summary statistics"

summary = (
    adata.obs.groupby("seurat_clusters", observed=True)
    .agg(
        n_cells=("seurat_clusters", "size"),
        mean_pseudotime=("velocity_pseudotime", "mean"),
        mean_latent_time=("latent_time", "mean"),
        mean_confidence=("velocity_confidence", "mean"),
    )
    .round(4)
)
# Sort clusters numerically (0, 1, 2, ... 10) instead of lexicographically
summary = summary.loc[sorted(summary.index, key=lambda x: int(x))]

HTML(
    summary.style
    .background_gradient(subset=["mean_latent_time"], cmap="viridis")
    .background_gradient(subset=["mean_confidence"], cmap="RdYlGn")
    .format(precision=4)
    .set_table_styles([
        {"selector": "th",
         "props": [("background-color", "#0E454A"),
                   ("color", "#fff"),
                   ("font-weight", "bold"),
                   ("padding", "12px 16px"),
                   ("font-size", "0.85rem")]},
        {"selector": "td",
         "props": [("border-bottom", "1px solid #e8f0f0"),
                   ("padding", "10px 16px")]},
        {"selector": "",
         "props": [("border-radius", "10px"),
                   ("overflow", "hidden"),
                   ("box-shadow", "0 2px 12px rgba(14,69,74,0.08)")]},
    ])
    .to_html()
)
```

---

## Interpretation of RNA Velocity Results

RNA velocity leverages the ratio of unspliced to spliced mRNA abundance to
infer the future transcriptional state of individual cells [@LaManno2018; @Bergen2020].
In the dynamical model, the kinetic parameters — transcription rate ($\alpha$),
splicing rate ($\beta$), and degradation rate ($\gamma$) — are estimated for
each gene via an expectation-maximisation algorithm, yielding a shared **latent
time** that orders cells along a continuous differentiation trajectory.

The velocity streamlines projected onto the UMAP embedding
(@fig-dyn-stream-umap-sc) reveal the predominant directionality of
transcriptional change across cell clusters.  Importantly, latent time
(@fig-latent-umap) is inferred independently of the UMAP coordinates; it is
derived solely from the gene-level kinetic fits.  When latent time aligns with
a gradient across the UMAP (e.g., early progenitors on one side, terminally
differentiated cells on the other), this provides strong orthogonal evidence
that the low-dimensional embedding faithfully captures the underlying
biological progression.  Conversely, clusters that appear adjacent in UMAP
space but differ markedly in latent time may indicate transitions that are
transcriptionally rapid yet involve large regulatory changes — a pattern
frequently observed at lineage commitment points.

The PAGA velocity graph (@fig-paga) provides a coarse-grained view of
cluster-cluster transitions, collapsing the cell-level velocity graph into
directed edges between clusters weighted by transition confidence.  This is
particularly useful for identifying the dominant differentiation routes in
complex trajectories with multiple branch points [@Wolf2019].

The per-cluster summary (@tbl-cluster-summary) includes both
`velocity_pseudotime` (from the stochastic model's transition probability
matrix) and `latent_time` (from the dynamical model).  Discrepancies between
the two can highlight genes or cell states where the steady-state assumption
breaks down and the full dynamical treatment is essential for accurate
trajectory inference [@Bergen2020].

---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Report generated by [ImmunoGeneTeqs, Inc.](https://en.immunogeneteqs.com/)** — Next Generation Sequencing Contract Analysis Service
:::

## Session Information

The following session information is provided for reproducibility of this report.

```{python}
#| label: session-info
#| echo: false

import sys
import platform
import os
import datetime as dt
import subprocess
from io import StringIO
import pandas as pd
from importlib import metadata as importlib_metadata
from IPython.display import HTML as IPyHTML, display


def _pkg_version(pkg_name):
    try:
        return importlib_metadata.version(pkg_name)
    except importlib_metadata.PackageNotFoundError:
        return "not installed"


def _pkg_version_alias(*names):
    for n in names:
        try:
            return importlib_metadata.version(n), n
        except importlib_metadata.PackageNotFoundError:
            continue
    return "not installed", "(none)"


session_df = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        ("Report timestamp (UTC)", dt.datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")),
        ("Local timezone", str(dt.datetime.now().astimezone().tzinfo)),
        ("Python", sys.version.replace("\n", " ")),
        ("Python executable", sys.executable),
        ("Conda env", os.environ.get("CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV", "not set")),
        ("Platform", platform.platform()),
        ("System", f"{platform.system()} {platform.release()}"),
        ("Machine", platform.machine()),
        ("Processor", platform.processor()),
        ("Current working directory", os.getcwd()),
    ],
    columns=["Item", "Value"],
)

display(IPyHTML(session_df.to_html(index=False)))

core_packages = [
    ("scvelo", ("scvelo",)),
    ("scanpy", ("scanpy",)),
    ("anndata", ("anndata",)),
    ("numpy", ("numpy",)),
    ("pandas", ("pandas",)),
    ("scipy", ("scipy",)),
    ("matplotlib", ("matplotlib",)),
    ("seaborn", ("seaborn",)),
    ("scikit-learn", ("scikit-learn",)),
    ("numba", ("numba",)),
    ("h5py", ("h5py",)),
    ("loompy", ("loompy",)),
    ("igraph (python-igraph)", ("igraph", "python-igraph")),
    ("louvain", ("louvain",)),
    ("umap-learn", ("umap-learn",)),
    ("pynndescent", ("pynndescent",)),
    ("joblib", ("joblib",)),
    ("statsmodels", ("statsmodels",)),
]
core_rows = []
for label, aliases in core_packages:
    ver, found_as = _pkg_version_alias(*aliases)
    core_rows.append((label, ver, found_as))
core_df = pd.DataFrame(
    core_rows,
    columns=["Package", "Version", "Resolved as"],
)
display(IPyHTML(core_df.to_html(index=False)))

# R session information
def _safe_html(text):
    return (
        str(text)
        .replace("&", "&amp;")
        .replace("<", "&lt;")
        .replace(">", "&gt;")
    )


display(IPyHTML("<h4>R Runtime</h4>"))
r_available = False
try:
    r_runtime_cmd = [
        "Rscript", "-e",
        (
            "si <- sessionInfo(); "
            "out <- data.frame("
            "Item=c('R','Platform','Running under','Time zone',"
            "'Attached base packages','Other attached packages'), "
            "Value=c("
            "R.version.string, "
            "si$platform, "
            "si$running, "
            "as.character(Sys.timezone()), "
            "paste(si$basePkgs, collapse=', '), "
            "if (length(si$otherPkgs)) paste(names(si$otherPkgs), collapse=', ') else '(none)'"
            "), "
            "stringsAsFactors=FALSE); "
            "write.table(out, sep='\\t', row.names=FALSE, quote=FALSE)"
        ),
    ]
    r_runtime = subprocess.run(
        r_runtime_cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    r_runtime_df = pd.read_csv(StringIO(r_runtime.stdout), sep="\t")
    display(IPyHTML(r_runtime_df.to_html(index=False)))
    r_available = True
except FileNotFoundError:
    display(IPyHTML("<p><em>Rscript not found; R session info unavailable.</em></p>"))
except subprocess.CalledProcessError as exc:
    msg = _safe_html(exc.stderr.strip().splitlines()[-1] if exc.stderr else str(exc))
    display(IPyHTML(f"<p><em>Failed to collect R runtime info: {msg}</em></p>"))

if r_available:
    display(IPyHTML("<h4>R Packages</h4>"))
    try:
        r_pkg_cmd = [
            "Rscript", "-e",
            (
                "ip <- as.data.frame(installed.packages()[, c('Package','Version')]); "
                "ip <- ip[order(tolower(ip$Package)), , drop=FALSE]; "
                "write.table(ip, sep='\\t', row.names=FALSE, quote=FALSE)"
            ),
        ]
        r_pkg = subprocess.run(
            r_pkg_cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
        )
        r_pkg_df = pd.read_csv(StringIO(r_pkg.stdout), sep="\t")
        display(
            IPyHTML(
                "<details><summary><strong>Complete R package list "
                f"({len(r_pkg_df)} packages)</strong></summary>"
                "<div style='max-height:420px; overflow:auto; margin-top:10px;'>"
                f"{r_pkg_df.to_html(index=False)}"
                "</div></details>"
            )
        )
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError as exc:
        msg = _safe_html(exc.stderr.strip().splitlines()[-1] if exc.stderr else str(exc))
        display(IPyHTML(f"<p><em>Failed to collect R packages: {msg}</em></p>"))

    try:
        r_si_cmd = [
            "Rscript", "-e",
            "cat(paste(capture.output(sessionInfo()), collapse='\\n'))",
        ]
        r_si = subprocess.run(
            r_si_cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
        )
        si_html = _safe_html(r_si.stdout)
        display(
            IPyHTML(
                "<details><summary><strong>Raw R sessionInfo()</strong></summary>"
                "<pre style='white-space:pre-wrap; max-height:420px; overflow:auto; "
                "margin-top:10px;'>" + si_html + "</pre></details>"
            )
        )
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError as exc:
        msg = _safe_html(exc.stderr.strip().splitlines()[-1] if exc.stderr else str(exc))
        display(IPyHTML(f"<p><em>Failed to collect raw sessionInfo(): {msg}</em></p>"))

# Full Python environment package list
pkg_versions = {}
for dist in importlib_metadata.distributions():
    name = dist.metadata.get("Name") if hasattr(dist, "metadata") else None
    if not name:
        name = getattr(dist, "name", None)
    if name:
        pkg_versions[name] = dist.version

all_df = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        sorted(pkg_versions.items(), key=lambda x: x[0].lower()),
        columns=["Package", "Version"],
    )
)
display(
    IPyHTML(
        "<details><summary><strong>Complete Python package list "
        f"({len(all_df)} packages)</strong></summary>"
        "<div style='max-height:420px; overflow:auto; margin-top:10px;'>"
        f"{all_df.to_html(index=False)}"
        "</div></details>"
    )
)
```

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
